speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Glenn Gilder took place at his home near Troy on May 22nd, 1975. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: My showmanship. He is a buffalo hunter, I think. They claim he hunted for the railroads, but then everybody hunted for the railroads.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I have an idea that if Bill Cotey went out to get a buffalo for the railroad, it probably took some Indian kid along to do the shooting and the skinning and he got the glory. I didn't like him. There was no sincerity to him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It's a funny thing that he's the one that people think about when they think about what the West was really like, you know?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, there hasn't been anybody who had more ballyhoo and he promoted his show from Texas to Dakota to Madison Square Garden to Europe and he was a showman, a promoter. He was in it for the glory of the almighty dollar. Well, that's my opinion. You know, somebody else got a different opinion. Maybe one of the others could be right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'll bet you're right. I'll bet you are. It's just funny. It seems to me like the story of what the West is really like is something that most people don't even know about. That's what they think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think the truth about the West is better than the fiction about it?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yes. Yes.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: The truth about the West doesn't end up on the basis of a lot of good novels. The funny thing is that it was made up and segregated into agriculture, mining, milling, fishing, logging, lumbering.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And all good subjects, if they're handled good, they make a good book. I like all those up there.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Some railroading, logging.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It seems to me that 95 % of the stuff about the West that you read is all about the cowboys and the cattle on the range. I can't quite figure that one out. Well, it was here.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: The eastern version of the West, he came through the prairies dates and seen that. There have been some remarkably good stories written on wheat farming and homesteading.

There was another one, a fellow by the name of Estes wrote a book about the country just south and east of Denver. One of the best examples I think I've read of it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder

words: I don't think any two stories are close to the same. Any two stories that I've heard, you know, everybody's life is different.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: More than you'd expect being from the same area. They live on adjoining farms and the interests be a little bit different. Well, that's the way it goes.

words: I would, and when you do, if you could get him with, uh, Frank Herzog. Get the Prune Joe story. Get the Pippen Gilbert story on cleaning up that meadow up there in Kenlan Homestead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what's that story?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: That's a good one. Well, tell me it. Well, it's so long ago since I've heard it. I don't know whether I can or not to get it exactly right, but, uh, when the Potlites first come into the country, they put a camp there close to Bennett Mill, south and up on the hill. And they put another one up there on the, uh, on a part of the land that Jess Euler owns that he got from Ben Stewart. Stewart got it from Bill Helmer. And that was camp three. And then camp four was less than a mile from there. I don't know why. Different time.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And, uh, Gil, evidently, was a foreman for the, kind of overall them. But they, they wanted to land their logs off of Jerome Creek on the bank of the Blue Silver. Well, that put it on Kenlan's place.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I don't know whether Gil didn't know who owned the land or what. But he went in there and started cutting the logs and they started landing them along the river there, cutting the brush and cleaning it all up. And they filled that meadow, as you can tell, with ten acres there, solid with logs. And they got ready to put the drive on. And Kenlan said, no, you can't move these logs until you pay for the privilege of putting them on that land. And by golly, they didn't. They cleaned his land and then they paid him for it, which is all exactly what I'd have done to, I think, under the circumstances.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Those things.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, who's this Prune Joe? Prune Joe?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah. He's Bill Helmer's ranch foreman.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Uh, you'd get that story from Frank. Frank's a little older and knew him a little better than I did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, tell me what you heard, anyway. Well, I didn't hear it. I knew the guy.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Why they called him Prune Joe, I don't know. I've heard a dozen different stories. He made wine out of prunes. He was an Austrian.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Or was it half of the diet? Prunes and beans?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I couldn't say, but that was his name. He went in there and cleared up all the land.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: For Bill Helmer, he cleaned up...

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: 120... Cleared up better than 200 acres out of heavy timber and put it in crop. But, of course, he had access to all the damn money he wanted. I don't know whether Bill Helmer had that much money or not, but there was always a crew there. A lot of men.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I don't know. I...

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: You'd get that story from Frank.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, Helmer was their main cruiser, right? Helmer was their main cruiser?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah, yeah. Head cruiser, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know what the story is on how the mill came to Potlatch?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, that's where Derry wanted it. You see, the mill was the first to come to Moscow.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And they were putting a railroad from Moscow... They were going to get up into the Boville area some way. And they'd done a lot of work on it. They made that cut where they had that second... Well, that sanitary landfill between Troy and Moscow. That was the cut for the company's railroad. And when they got that far,

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: they were having a meeting in Moscow. It was raining and Bill Helmer came in there and Derry... I guess they were sopping wet they'd been out on the brush all day. And Derry was kind of angry. He told them to stop it. He took a map, he poked it down through that... He poked a pencil down through that map and he says, That mill was going to be right there and he poked it down where Potlatch is. We had a grade clear over there.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: This going from Moscow to Boville wouldn't have got any excessive amount of timber. But going from Palouse to Boville, they went through the whole thing. It was not a grade practically.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: So that's where the railroad went and that's where the mill went.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't you tell me he had a story? I mean, didn't he have a reason about it? About Moscow? Didn't you tell me in Moscow there wasn't enough water in Moscow?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: There wasn't a pond, there wasn't anything there to influence it. No, Derry was right.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: You know, this is all written up.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: By who or what end?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I don't know, I don't remember. The Miller boat couldn't.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Possibly, part of it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: The fellow that told me this story was the engineer.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: His name was Talbot. He's dead now. He told me that story about 10 or 15 years ago. He was there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Before they started the town there at Potlatch, you were telling me that Palouse was really the big center. It was really an important place for the people in the country. Well, it was. It was the only.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: There was a stage stop in the post office at Woodfell, a stage stop in the post office at Hampton, and one at Kennedy Ford. There used to be little. I guess they handled the mail and probably, part of the time, a few groceries. But if you wanted any groceries and lived at Harvard or Avon or whatever, you had to go to Flus after it. Dover team.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Sometimes that took quite a while. Some of them had teams that go down there pretty darn quick.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Go from Harvard to Potlatch and back and do their shopping in two hours and a half. Can't do that with a Model T Ford.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, that's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that road like when you were growing up there?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: About six inches wider than a wagon. Mud axle deep all winter and dust halfway up all summer.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: There were a few days when it was pretty nice, like early in the spring, late in the fall.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Some of those horses made some awful runs. Accidents. Things like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean running into each other on that road?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No, no. Say that somebody got hurt and they sent somebody after a doctor. It didn't take them long to go twenty -five miles. They just kicked that horse in high gear and he had to butt them and stamina to stay and make the trip. When he got that one made, he took a feed oats and a drink of water and went back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What made your parents decide to come out here to this area?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you come from?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: My mother came from Iowa. She was just a little girl when she came, I guess. A young girl. My dad must have been about nineteen, eighteen or nineteen years old. He came from Sault Ste. Marie to Dakota.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: He didn't like it. He'd heard about the Palouse Country, so he came on the Palouse.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: He had itchy feet, I guess. That's about the only reason I know of.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: The West had been told and played up by the railroads and people that had gone back. It was so fantastically wonderful. Every Easterner would have came out here if he could have.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder why he didn't like Dakota.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh, oh, droughts and grasshoppers kicked him out of there. There was nothing left after two things got through with him.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh, I don't know. The three boys, four of them, came west. One came to Sweetgrass in Montana and stayed there. They had a very successful life. Charlie came to Palouse and Albin because Dad had came, partly.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And then one went on over to Vancouver.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About when did your father get out here, you know?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Must have been about 90 or 91.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: My mother, her folks, must have come out about 85 or somewhere around in that area.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who is that?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: She was born in 87, maybe about 85 or 86. Well, some person in there. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Seems like this country was one of the last pieces that was real good land to get settled up. I mean, in the Palouse, the whole Palouse country. Sure a lot better than Dakota.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, I think that's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Lorraine, huh?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yes. It was kind of slow getting in here, but when they did get there, they stayed.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: There's a book up there, Whitman County History, that's awful good. It tells the dates and all these things.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Settling Pullman and Union Flat.

words: Your folks didn't move up by Harvard at first, right? They were someplace?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No, they had homesteaded down at Hooper.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Hooper. About, oh, maybe 10 miles on the side of the cross.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And he stayed there five or six years and broke up that prairie.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, he knew he didn't have very good wheat land and he wanted to raise wheat. So he put in two or three crops that weren't very good and finally he got a good crop. And that crop sold a place, so he got out of there and came up to Palouse. And then up to Harvard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he buy the place there or did he, he bought it?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Uh huh.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: He bought 80 acres from Potlice Lumber Company and 120 from Bill Helmer.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, it was logged over, it was mostly brush, he cleared it up.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: The hard way with team of horses and...

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh, team of horses and a grubble.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you get to do much of that yourself?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Okay.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: You betcha. A boy was a man about that time when he was about 10 or 11, 12 years old. He could drive a team where he was as good as a man.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: That's the way it worked.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: You can take them along to know how to run one end of a crosscut saw.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But the next thing he got to be expert with was an axe. He could just about plane a board with an axe.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can I have a cigarette, Glenn?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh, you sure can. You betcha.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But we just made kind of a general plan and traded out if we could, when we could.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Sometimes they'd get a piece of grum cleared up way up in June. They'd get through with it, you know, maybe a half an acre or an acre. It's too late to put it in grain. They'd plow it and put it in spuds or somewhere to follow it. Put it in crop next year.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: On a tiny scale it was just like they worked it after they got on a bigger scale. Only it was a tiny scale, I'm telling you. Half an acre was important.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, what do you think of it?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: It'd be in a fence corner.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Seems like now the farmer's got to put every square inch under if he can.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah, they're doing it. But we... My dad had the idea that another acre of wheat was a way of getting there.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I've never agreed with him. I never have seen the day when I considered wheat a good crop. I don't know why. I'd sooner have hay or a cow or some other way. But I just... In my life I just trusted wheat. I thought it was unreliable. It was unsecure. Unless you could just be in the very biggest bracket. And it's probably partly the reason some of the very biggest ones. During the 1920s and long in there were the first ones to go broke. At least they had wonderful auction sales. Thirty, fifty head horses. All their harness and gear and rigging and equipment.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: A good time was had by all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The wheat market went down fast after the first war, right?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: After all wars. I think the country got hurt the worst after World War I. Unless it's being hurt the worst right now.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But your father believed in wheat. Probably most people did.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No, not most. They all... That was a cash crop. Grain. Grain. Grain.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And they'd come here and bust it the next. Make it again the next year and bust the next. Up and down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father work out when you were a kid?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Everybody did.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: He worked at any job he could find around close to home.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Maybe a day or two or maybe a month. There wasn't any work in the wintertime. Seriously ever.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But during that time he'd build or make friends. Do something. He never was idle, that's for damn sure.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: He'd be more ambitious than I've ever been. He'd be ready to start at six o 'clock in the morning. He wasn't ready to quit until six o 'clock at night either.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I've never been... I don't know. I've never been... That much to crowd things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many brothers and sisters? How big was the family?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Eight.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wouldn't it?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: One, two... No, wait a minute. Six. There were three girls and three boys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, two parents.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I was thinking of my own family.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: There was a hard deal to support that many.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: A kid nowadays in school has more laid out on him in one year than that whole family had in two.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But we didn't miss anything. We didn't... Somehow or other you can't miss anything that you don't know exists.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: What is it? How are you going to say it?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Ignorance is bliss.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: We should have blessed her then.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, we had food and clothes.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: There was a shortage of that. But the clothes weren't fancy. They were just common everyday old work clothes. But they were warm and most of the time dry.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Ten dollars would buy all the clothes I needed for winter when I was about twelve, thirteen years old. I know because I earned that much and bought my own clothes. I had all I needed.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Of course there was a difference between a tuxedo and a pair of overalls.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do to earn that money?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I generally drove horses for somebody putting in a crop.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Or anything. Not the way those kids did earn money though. They'd drive horses for somebody.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: They'd sit on the seat you know. I had nothing to see a twelve, thirteen year old kid driving six or eight horses. I could handle them just as good as a great grandpa.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But anyway, they didn't have to have six or eight if they could. Two or any unit of it. It depends on the size of the operation and where it was. They didn't have six horse teams around Harvard but they did around below potlatch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you have in a way a stock when you were ten or a youngster?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: About three horses and twelve cows I imagine. Maybe two or three brutes out and a few chickens. That's about it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Then they got more as the place grew larger as the cleared land increased. Each of those things increased as there was feed for them.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But it started small and had to start small. You couldn't buy hay, you couldn't buy grain.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: In the first place you didn't have the money and in the second place it wasn't for sale.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say it was pretty much self sufficient? The farm?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Just as near as possible. Yes.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And it was pretty much possible.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: We take our own wheat to a mill someplace. At first they didn't grind it only just into a good grade of hog feed but they chopped and made food.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I think they took a sack of wheat to Palouse to Old Man Hunsberger there in the grist mill. I think he sent them back a sack of flour, fifty pounds, for each sack of wheat. He also sent back a farina and bran.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: That was their flour. Just practically growing on the place. Spuds, milk, butter, eggs, vegetables.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: There wasn't much to buy. Coffee, sugar, tomato and salt most of the time.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Bacon powder with awful expense. About fifty cents a can for a two or three pound can.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: It was a good life time. I don't think any of us got any kick or any rugrats on it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: We did our work and we had our time to play just the same as we've got now I think even more.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: We made our own amusement, dances, parties, swimming, sleigh rides.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: My brother and I used to like to walk and climb a hill the highest hill we could see. It didn't matter if it was ten miles away we'd climb it. We'd go fishing, hunting, trapping, those. We found a lot of things. Then there were just everyday occurrences that people pay two hundred and fifty dollars a week to do now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what they do?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Come out from the city and pay two hundred and three hundred and fifty dollars a week to do what we played at as kids. And all of our life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You did trapping huh?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Frank Herzog, he was a better trapper than I am. He got back and got the bobcats and bear. His dad got fifteen one summer. Fifteen. Bear, big ones.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: They used to be thick.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: His dad used to come by our place on his way to and from his traps and he'd always leave a quarter of a bear steak for us. About once a week.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: He caught a bobcat one time and he had it in his head and he was going to take it home to skin it. The damn thing come to in the sack and scratched him a little bit.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: He just went on home with it and put a collar around his neck and tied it in the back of the woodshed and that cat was around there for a long time.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But the kids used to, we used to trap muskrats and mink. A few coyotes, quite a few weasels when they get white. And they made fifteen, twenty dollars in a winter's time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How'd you get around mostly in the winter when you were a kid Gwen?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: On Shanks Funnies.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Of course we had our saddle horses too but any horses we had were certainly broke to ride and they furnished our transportation.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But mostly we walked or hell we seldom walk we run.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: If I was going to go to Troy when I was fifteen or eighteen years old I wouldn't walk. I'd run two thirds away in there. Take nothing of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would you go?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Great Sioux. Didn't follow the roads. Go straight Sioux.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: When we had these coyote traps back up there, there was three of us in on it and we'd run. Seventy eight miles around the string. And each of us had stepped in the guy ahead of him's tracks without disturbing that track. That was a game.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: You'd get pretty darned expert at it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In winter did you snowshoe much?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Some.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: If needed be. We generally had snowshoes or skis but we made them ourselves if we did have.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This route you'd take to Troy from Harvard that interests me. How you'd go over the divide there and get into town here. I know it's not very far by the land but it sure is far nowadays.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: When I moved over here to this place I moved from Harvard here.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: The road came just as it does now but after you got up there, or while I prayed we used to live, the road cut through by white pine mill and we'd come up right in front of Hans' house.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And then we'd have to go down the highway and down to Norquake and then get off the highway and come in here. But I moved the whole outfit with accepting I think a load of stock and a load or two hay. Anyway I moved it all of course, household goods and foolishly moved my old junk machinery over here which I shouldn't have done. I should have sold it over there and bought it back over here because that would have been possible. Didn't need much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What year was this when you moved here? Here?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: There was no snow there, no sleighing. It was all wagon. One morning I thought I had sleighing enough to get a load over here and I loaded the sled and pulled out over there. And I got to Avon, up this side of Avon and the snow just simply gave out. It melted.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And I struggled all day to get up there just about where Garrelle Price lives and unhooked, left my sled setting and went back and stayed all night with Dwight Corey and got a team from him the next morning and come on and move that rig on over here. And I took the wagon for the balance of it. There wasn't any snow, the ground didn't get white all winter. Two or three days. And the next winter it made up for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the fishing up there when you were a kid?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: It was good.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: It was real good. Any place.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: You never got big faced. Oh, 16, 18 inches long but there was... You got enough in a little while to have a good meal on them.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: We used to go up to Strychnine and get in the creek and wade down to Third Park and get out and take and wrap the fish in a couple of, three or four thimbleberry leaves. And just make a mud bowl around him. Put him in the coals. Put a spud in there with him, alongside of him in the ashes. Covered up good. Egg or two. It was a pretty good meal. In the afternoon we'd probably fish on down to a couple of miles. But we'd have nice messes. The meat was mostly about 8 -10 inches long.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I think the meat was just yellow salmon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had that country up in there been logged when you were a kid?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No, they were logging it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Of course they got done around Harvard. They got pretty well done by about 1910 or 1912. The pot lights had pulled out of there and gone up. Old Helmer up in that area.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Boville. But they had, there was always some logging going on. After we kids got a little size on us, what we used to saw during the summer for some of those little loggers around close.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We used to run into those old miners back in that country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Like hoodoos?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Sure. Sure, we loved them. Those kids did.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Steffens, Billy, Connors, Pete Dufner, Airsmith, Jack Connors. We used to go up there and work for them. Lou Watson did the assessment work for some of them. For Lou Watson we did several years.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But they were old bachelors and they liked kids. And on Friday night after school we'd quite often go up there and stay Friday night, Saturday night. And then Sunday we walked back home.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: We had some good friends up there among them old fellows. They liked to see us kids coming. And us kids liked to go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they mind having you around their diggings and stuff like that?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh no, we didn't bother them.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: We soon learned that there were limitations of what privileges we had while we were visiting one of them. But we'd just play around the woods or cut woods for them. It looked like if anything needed to be done we'd do it, you know.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: They'd visit with us. I think they enjoyed it. I know they did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Those guys must have been pretty lonely by themselves sometimes being alone and all that.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And then bother them. That's the way they wanted it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No, they'd live there within a quarter of a mile or half a mile of each other and wouldn't go in each other's cabin once a year. Most of them.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: We used to get paid pretty good to take a team and wagon and take their groceries up to them once or twice a year. We used to do that with Doffner.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Once or twice with Steffens and for Lou Watson. We used to have a regular run there. We'd have to go up there about every two or three weeks all summer. Go up one day and back the next.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just your family? Your family?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No, no. Just one or two of us boys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where were they located? The miners that you're speaking of? Have you been up?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I know the area pretty well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know where the North Fork and the South Fork of the Palouse Fork up there?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Sure. Well, there was one below that fork about a half a mile or a little bit near by Livingston. And then the rest of them were at the North Fork.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And this one back in there where Whedon saw the thing and they were about a mile. And then Steffens and Doffner and O 'Connor just scattered along.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they do very good up there mining? Hmm? Did they do very good up there mining?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Doug Heirsmith told me one time that they took out six dollars a day while they could wash on their claim. Thought it would be two of them working. That was pretty good money then.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: The rest of them were mum. You didn't know what they did. I'll never tell you.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: They didn't leave any tremendous estates, so I know that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think, Glenn, that they were different from the real kind of difference in the kind of people they were from the settlers? The kind of people that settled and had farms? Were they a special breed?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No, I don't think so. They just wanted to be independents of the rest of the world. And they sure as hell were.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No, they weren't. They just didn't marry. That's about the only difference I can see in them. Oh, there was two of them. John English and Jack Connor. Jean Wiedenthal. Three of them got married. And then, Livingston was married when he came in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think they were different?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Ted,

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I can't think of his name. He lived south of town about three or four miles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Strome?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You've got to tell me what you mean by independent.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They didn't want to be messed with more than most people?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, I think they had that, but they didn't want to be dependent on anybody or anything.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And I don't think they wanted anybody to be dependent on them from the way it worked out.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: They were nice old guys.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Cranky sometimes.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Most of the time, though.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I'll tell you, they were cranky with each other.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But, I never seen a day when they weren't friendly with my brothers or I or our families. When they happened to be up in that area hookle -bearing or fishing or something like that.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Sometimes they'd go out with us and show us where there's a good patch or... I'm glad to see us, I think. I acted like they were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, those men stayed up in there for many years, didn't they?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think they've had among themselves many stories about hidden gold, lost mines?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No, I don't. No. Well, there was a doctor in Palouse had a lost mine, a lost wheelbarrow mine up there on the west side of Gold Hill. He blew that up pretty big, but actually it was just an abandoned mine and there was no great lot of ore there. The Caricos and Gillums and others were in there.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: There was lots of prospecting done.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: For deep mines, after the gold rush was over, you see, there was quite a little gold rush in that area at first. And then after the country was worked out, there were some deep prospects went in. One on Baby Grand and the Metis Paul and the Gold Bug.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And the Caricos, or whoever that lost wheelbarrow was, I don't remember. But they were deep.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But they never hit enough to justify their keeping going.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You knew him from hell, didn't you?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Real well. He was in the Gold Bug.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the deal on him?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They say he had quite a bit of worthless mining stock that a lot of people bought. Nobody wants to admit they did buy it themselves, but they say everybody else did.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, the Gold Bug showed pretty good prospecting to miners. I wouldn't know. Nothing about it. But Ed... There were quite a number involved in it. They actually believed in it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And they worked it for years. And when they get short of money, they go out and sell stock.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And they work that up and heat it up and go sell some more.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But it never turned out well. But the Northups in the Gold Bug, they worked the same deal. They didn't have money enough to develop it. They had a faith that it was going to eventually turn good. And they worked for years and get every dollar they could. Selling stock or trading, dickering. To keep going. Keep going down, down. But it just... both of these places shipped ore.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And that's bought quite a bit. But they couldn't make it pay. It wasn't rich enough. Too expensive to mine it, perhaps.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Those men... works up in the Bunker Hill.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Marvin Darryl.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And he still has a stock in the Mezpah and some several claims in there on Poor Man Creek. He spent his life in mining in the big mines up there. And he still got his confidence and faith in it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, who knows?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Someday they might.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Marvin Darryl is a man that... I think he knows what he's talking about. He's not trying to sell me any. The fact is, I don't think he would sell me any. But he gets all of it he can get his hands on.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And there's others up there right in with him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The Mezpah took quite a bit of copper out of there, didn't they?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah. The Alafilah hauled it out with a six horse team wagon. Trail wagon.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I don't think. I don't know. Maybe they trucked some of it out. But I think it was all done before they were trucks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, Glenn, what's the story that you know about that Duff Wagner feud on the creek there?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, I don't think anybody knows too much about that.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But old Billy Deaf was sleeping sound as could be. And old Wagner Charlie came in and opened the door and jumped on top of him. If I had one shot down the long side of his head, he'd have done a star beating him with that pistol.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Now, I don't know what caused that. I knew both of them and I've talked to both of them about it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I don't think they had had any argument before that. I don't think they'd had any particular trouble.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: They never I can't see any justifiable reason for it happening, but that's just what happened. And after he beat him a little bit, he got up off him, went back outside and went home and I don't think there was two words said all the time he was in there. And Duff's what it went for him. I had him arrested and they had a trial over it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Duff packed a pistol on his hip though from that day till the day he died. He put that on the first thing he got up in the morning and he slept with it under his pillow.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But they never had any more trouble. That was it. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Neither of them ever told you their side of it?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: A Wagner wouldn't say very much. He didn't say, well nothing of any consequence. He didn't want to talk about it. Duff didn't mind. But that's just about all the story that Duff told. He was back to visit. He used to come and get me and my team or my dad's team, whichever were available. Every spring. And we'd repair all the roads between

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, between where the highway is now, there are Kinmans and Cohens. Into the head of the roads, both on the heads of the Palouse River. And we'd grade that road with a little old horse grader that you could put in there and pick up and carry. Four horses on it. I drove them. And we'd fix a culvert here and maybe a ditch there. Maybe take out a stump someplace. But, well, it generally took about 10 -12 days to repair that road all up real good. I don't remember what I got for wages. $3 or $4 a day I think for me and the team.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Pretty good steak.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the county pay for that?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mm -hmm.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I had to walk down to Princeton to get my check for that. Old name of Hawkins was a treasurer of the road district for a few years. And then I think he died and I thought of my name while Lenhart took it over then. But I know many times I've walked down from home to Princeton to get that check.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just where was the home place from Harvard?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: You know when you're driving out going to Mida, you go up from the time you leave the store there at Harvard, you go up quite a long grade. Just a slight little grade. When you get to the top of that, you look straight east a mile and that's it. There's a White House and Big Barn, just oilers down to the hill and then my folks place was back on top of the hill.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: August Karl Mance.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: His mother owned that now.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: There's just two farms in that area now where there used to be one, two, three, four, there used to be five.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Two guys who got the whole country in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a real community when you were a kid growing up there?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah, Harvard was a hub. There was one pretty darn nice store, hotel,

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: blacksmith shop, liver stable and an ice cream parlor. That was it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But it was a community center and about one or two nights a week there'd be something going on, something social. They had a good range,

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: dances, parties, literaries. They had the church in the school house until, gee I believe it must have been pretty near 1925 or such a matter before they built a church up there.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: They were a religious community but they never had money to build a church I guess. They were always a school house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is the school house where the community get togethers were mostly?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: There or the Grange Hall, yeah. That's the only two places there were.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Grange and dances and parties, literary.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: There was always something. They had a lot of good times.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No friction, they all enjoyed themselves. I don't think there was such a thing as jealousy or friction in the entire neighborhood.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: For a Grange Master I liked just about anybody, this one, always a different one. They were always satisfied with the way it went. The church in Sunday School was about the same way. I never heard any arguments or anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Hi, how are you doing?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We need to have parties.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would they be like big fiends too?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh yeah, sure.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Fourth of July and Lincoln's birthday and Christmas.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And when anything going on like that, there didn't just a fugle. Everybody in the neighborhood went. If there was good skating, and there used to be some tremendous skating, about six o 'clock there would be 150 people skating around there. Come from every direction. Believe me, them people that grew up around the Great Lakes or up in Minnesota, those states, talk about skaters, they don't have many better than the shows they have there in Spokane.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would one be like? Like everybody would skate until dark and then go in and have a big feed, that kind of thing?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Just about anything you can imagine. They might go and play games for a while.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They have those party games like Skip to Malu and things like that?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: They had to think of something and they thought of everything.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Crazy games, all kinds, anything.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Spin the platter and what the heck, heavy, heavy hangs over your head. I see a bunch of people in there all ages playing games that were invented for kids, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But don't it have fun?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Sleigh rides in the wintertime. I tried to get Carl to tell you about a sleigh ride one night.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he tell you about having a chicken feed at the end of it?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, I can't remember now what it was that he said. I know.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Just involve chicken feed to what they do now and of course they had to solve all these pros and cons for everybody that's running for office.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Price of wheat in the war news, just the same as it is now. The only thing is now, you see one or two guys at a time, they used to get in there, ten or fifteen of them.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I don't see how they got so much to talk about out of them two little papers, but they did.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Now we got the television and radio papers.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Don't seem to cover it any better.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But I do think that the things that they cover now are more disgusting, discouraging and disturbing than they were then.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But they had the trends, it was similar.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think they thought about prohibition?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That always seemed like such a dumb one to me.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: You know, it never affected that area up there very much.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: The dances were seldom, if ever, anybody took a bottle to them. And there were, I think there were two stills in the area, but the market was outside of the area.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But they never got excited, there wasn't a drinking crowd up there, there was a few of them that had to take a drink. Most any of them, they might make some wine or, but they didn't take it very seriously, there were no serious drinkers up there.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But you get down to Potlatch and it changed, it was different, they were pretty serious about it down there. But anyway, there was two stills in that country up there, and I don't think there ever was any more. If there had been, I'd known about it. By the gods around Potlatch and the rest of Latore County, there was one in almost every section.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Once in a great while, some of us kids would get a hold of a bottle full of white meal, and probably about 15 or 20 of us would smell the cork and be drunker in hell. But that's about all I'm up to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just a second, is Sue here?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah. Upstairs, just a second.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No, that was the community up there.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Sue? Sue?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: A hundred percent.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you hear her up there?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think maybe she might have gone out. A rosemary I think went someplace. She's not in the house now and I don't know where she is.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Okay. Well, who it is, call her back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about young people going together?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Many marriages come out of the young people in the community, meeting each other up there.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh yeah, sure.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Most of them. Married right around the community there. A few of us didn't. I went clear down to Potlatch.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Most of them got their girls closer to home.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: A lot of rivalry. Some friction. A few fights, but not too many.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh yeah, there are many, many, many of them. People around there. Amott, Cochran, Canfield.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh heck, I assume most of them. Ninety percent of them are just married right in the neighborhood.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the courting like in those days?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, I think it was just about the same as it is now. It either walked or drove a team in buggy.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Went to dances, shows, picnics, whatever. Church, mostly that. I was important. Church was important. That's where all the big girls went.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: So all the big boys did too.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I don't think that's changed too much. Means of transportation is about all. And the cost.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Used to cost four bits to take a girl out for nights in her attainment. Now they say it takes fifty dollars. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They don't shivaree anymore.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: That's too bad.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Boy, they sure shivareed us. Damn near tore our house down. It was just an old tie paper shack. I thought they were going to tear it down. I had a bucket of milk. I just got through milking. It was set on a drain board. I hadn't skimmed it yet. About three gallons of it. And a couple of guys got back behind that with a fence post that was banging on the wall.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And they tipped that bucket of milk over on the kitchen floor.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And Agnes and I had seen them coming. We weren't in the house. We were out with the shivaree crowd and they didn't know it. We were just a shivaree and Glenn and Agnes to beat the band. But when they tipped that bucket of milk over on the floor the whole crowd tripped right in. Waited through it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Put in an evening of having a lot of fun.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But the house was a mess.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Three gallons of milk goes quite a ways mixed with the mud.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: They all met up down to the highway I guess. I don't know. But anyway we seen them coming and we went outside and hid. And when they came up in the yard with them we just mixed right in with them. It was dark. They couldn't tell the difference. We were just a shivaree and to beat the band.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: That was part of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah it seems like a good custom to me.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And then they had their showers for their kids.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh I don't know. They had fun. They were hard. They were honest.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I think there was... I didn't know anybody on the Palouse River that wasn't admirable.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That speaks real highly of people.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: That's the way they were.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: We Palouse see each other now from Lewiston to Spokane or wherever. They're scattered.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: About two or three times a year one of them tell me that... Or tell my friends that were with me, don't ever believe that fella. He'd drink a drink out of the Palouse River and anybody that drinks out of the Palouse River will never tell the truth again.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But by golly, if a man gave you his word up there, you could take it if it was for a dollar or a thousand or whatever it was. You wouldn't need a note.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I suppose I should be getting going.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, I suppose you... I hope I've helped.
